questions.question: What is the cardinal symptom of acute anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Severe eye pain, along with photophobia and red eye, is the main symptom of acute anterior uveitis due to inflammation of the iris and ciliary body.

questions.question: Which slit lamp finding is characteristic of chronic anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Posterior synechiae, adhesions of the iris to the lens, are typical of chronic anterior uveitis due to persistent inflammation and fibrin formation.

questions.question: What is the typical duration of an episode of uncomplicated acute anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Acute anterior uveitis usually resolves in less than 6 weeks with treatment, while chronic uveitis persists for more than 3 months or recurs rapidly.

questions.question: Which topical agent is the first-line treatment in acute anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Topical corticosteroids, such as prednisolone acetate 1%, are the mainstay of treatment to suppress inflammation in acute anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What complication is more common in chronic anterior uveitis than in acute uveitis?
questions.explanation: Complicated cataract, due to persistent inflammation and prolonged use of corticosteroids, is a typical complication of chronic anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What systemic disease is most associated with recurrent acute anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis, an HLA-B27-positive spondyloarthropathy, is the most common systemic cause of recurrent acute anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic clinical sign of granulomatous anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Mutton-fat keratic precipitates are the typical finding of granulomatous anterior uveitis, formed by the accumulation of macrophages and epithelioid cells.

questions.question: What type of inflammation characterizes nongranulomatous anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Non-granulomatous anterior uveitis is characterized by diffuse lymphocytic inflammation, without granuloma formation, unlike granulomatous uveitis, which presents aggregates of epithelioid macrophages.

questions.question: What is a common cause of granulomatous anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Sarcoidosis is a classic cause of granulomatous anterior uveitis, along with tuberculosis, syphilis, and Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease. Spondyloarthropathies usually cause nongranulomatous uveitis.

questions.question: What type of anterior uveitis is typically associated with HLA-B27?
questions.explanation: Acute nongranulomatous anterior uveitis is strongly associated with HLA-B27, typically presenting as a unilateral, recurrent episode with pain, photophobia, and blurred vision.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for mild nongranulomatous anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Initial treatment of mild nongranulomatous anterior uveitis consists of topical corticosteroids (prednisolone) along with cycloplegics to prevent posterior synechiae and relieve pain.

questions.question: What complication is most common in untreated granulomatous anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: In untreated chronic granulomatous anterior uveitis, posterior synechiae formation is very common, which can lead to secondary glaucoma and complicated cataract.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic clinical sign of Fuchs syndrome?
questions.explanation: Heterochromia iridiana, with the lighter affected iris, is the most distinctive finding in Fuchs syndrome, a unilateral chronic anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What is the most common complication of Fuchs syndrome?
questions.explanation: Secondary glaucoma is the most common complication of Fuchs syndrome, due to trabecular obstruction by inflammatory cells and pigment debris.

questions.question: What type of cells are typically seen in the corneal endothelium in Fuchs syndrome?
questions.explanation: Fine keratic precipitates, diffusely distributed in the corneal endothelium, are characteristic of Fuchs syndrome and do not usually converge.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for Fuchs syndrome uveitis?
questions.explanation: Fuchs syndrome responds poorly to corticosteroids; Low doses are used only if there is symptomatic inflammation, avoiding aggressive treatments that do not modify the course.

questions.question: Which finding on gonioscopy is typical of Fuchs syndrome?
questions.explanation: The presence of anomalous vessels in the iridocorneal angle (gonioscopy) is a classic finding of Fuchs syndrome, indicating chronic low-grade inflammation.

questions.question: What characteristic differentiates Fuchs syndrome from other anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Fuchs syndrome presents with asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic chronic inflammation, without pain or photophobia, unlike other acute anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What is the typical presentation of anterior uveitis in juvenile idiopathic arthritis?
questions.explanation: Anterior uveitis associated with juvenile idiopathic arthritis is usually asymptomatic or painless, with mild red eye, detected in routine ophthalmological screening.

questions.question: What risk factor is most frequently associated with anterior uveitis in juvenile idiopathic arthritis?
questions.explanation: Positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA), especially in girls with oligoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis, are the main risk factor for developing anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for active anterior uveitis in juvenile idiopathic arthritis?
questions.explanation: Topical corticosteroids (such as 1% prednisolone acetate) are the standard initial treatment to control inflammation in active anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What serious complication can occur from uncontrolled anterior uveitis in juvenile idiopathic arthritis?
questions.explanation: Chronic inflammation can cause cataract (due to corticosteroids or inflammation) and secondary glaucoma, main causes of visual loss in these patients.

questions.question: Anterior uveitis is most associated with which subtype of juvenile idiopathic arthritis?
questions.explanation: The persistent oligoarticular form (involvement of ≤4 joints) is the one with the highest risk of anterior uveitis, especially in ANA-positive girls.

questions.question: What is the recommended frequency of ophthalmological screening in a girl with oligoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis and a positive ANA?
questions.explanation: Due to the high risk of asymptomatic uveitis, screening is recommended every 3 months in patients with positive and oligoarticular ANA, according to rheumatological guidelines.

questions.question: What is the most common genetic association in acute anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Acute anterior uveitis is strongly associated with the HLA-B27 antigen, present in approximately 50% of cases, especially in patients with ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.question: What disease is classically associated with anterior uveitis and HLA-B27 positivity?
questions.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis is the spondyloarthropathy most frequently associated with HLA-B27 positive acute anterior uveitis, occurring in up to 30-40% of patients.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for acute anterior uveitis in a patient with ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate) are the first-line treatment for acute anterior uveitis, as they control local inflammation quickly and safely.

questions.question: What typical slit lamp finding is seen in HLA-B27-associated anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Posterior synechiae (adhesions of the iris to the lens) are a characteristic finding of acute anterior uveitis, especially in HLA-B27 positive patients, due to the intense inflammatory reaction.

questions.question: In which age group is HLA-B27-associated anterior uveitis most common?
questions.explanation: HLA-B27 positive anterior uveitis predominates in young adults (20-40 years), coinciding with the typical age of onset of ankylosing spondylitis and other spondyloarthropathies.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism is proposed in HLA-B27-associated anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: It is postulated that HLA-B27 presents abnormal peptides that activate cytotoxic CD8 T lymphocytes, triggering an inflammatory response in the uveal tract, although the exact mechanism is not completely understood.

questions.question: What is the classic triad of Reiter syndrome?
questions.explanation: The classic triad of Reiter syndrome includes urethritis, conjunctivitis, and arthritis, although anterior uveitis is a common ocular manifestation in the chronic form.

questions.question: What infectious agent is most frequently associated with Reiter syndrome?
questions.explanation: Chlamydia trachomatis is the pathogen most frequently implicated in the triggering of Reiter's syndrome, a reactive arthritis that can be accompanied by anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What laboratory finding is characteristic in patients with Reiter syndrome?
questions.explanation: The association with HLA-B27 is very strong in Reiter syndrome, present in up to 80% of cases, which differentiates it from other inflammatory arthritis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for anterior uveitis in Reiter syndrome?
questions.explanation: Acute anterior uveitis is initially managed with topical corticosteroids to control inflammation and cycloplegics to prevent synechiae and relieve pain.

questions.question: Which joint is most commonly affected in Reiter syndrome arthritis?
questions.explanation: The arthritis of Reiter syndrome is usually asymmetrical oligoarticular, preferentially affecting the joints of the lower extremities, especially the knee.

questions.question: Which of the following skin manifestations is typical of Reiter syndrome?
questions.explanation: Blenorrhagic keratoderma is a skin lesion characteristic of Reiter's syndrome, which presents as hyperkeratotic papules on the palms and soles.

questions.question: What is the causative agent of syphilitic anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Syphilitic anterior uveitis is caused by Treponema pallidum, a spirochete that can invade the eye during secondary or tertiary syphilis.

questions.question: What ocular manifestation is characteristic of secondary syphilis?
questions.explanation: In secondary syphilis, granulomatous anterior uveitis is a classic ocular manifestation, often bilateral and associated with skin rash and lymphadenopathy.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for syphilitic anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Benzathine penicillin G is the treatment of choice for syphilis, including syphilitic anterior uveitis, due to its effectiveness against Treponema pallidum.

questions.question: What serologic test is used to confirm active syphilis in anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: The VDRL (nontreponemal) and FTA-ABS (treponemal) serologic tests are used together to confirm active syphilis; PCR is not standard nor is culture routine.

questions.question: Which ophthalmoscopic finding is typical in syphilitic anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Granulomatous (greasy) keratic precipitates are characteristic of granulomatous anterior uveitis due to syphilis, along with posterior synechiae and cells in the anterior chamber.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of syphilitic anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Syphilitic anterior uveitis is due to the direct invasion of Treponema pallidum into the uveal tissue, triggering a granulomatous inflammatory response.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism by which herpes simplex virus causes anterior uveitis?
options: Latency in trigeminal ganglion with reactivation
Direct infection of the corneal epithelium
questions.explanation: The herpes simplex virus remains latent in the trigeminal ganglion and its reactivation travels through the nerve fibers to the anterior segment of the eye, triggering anterior uveitis.

questions.question: Which clinical finding is characteristic of anterior uveitis due to ophthalmic herpes zoster?
questions.explanation: Reactivation of herpes zoster in the first trigeminal division (V1) produces an ipsilateral vesicular eruption, which is a pathognomonic sign of anterior uveitis due to ophthalmic herpes zoster.

questions.question: What is the first-line antiviral treatment for herpes simplex anterior uveitis?
options: Oral acyclovir 400 mg five times a day
questions.explanation: Oral acyclovir at a dose of 400 mg five times a day is the first-line antiviral treatment for anterior uveitis due to herpes simplex, demonstrating efficacy in reducing recurrence and inflammation.

questions.question: What complication is most frequently associated with anterior uveitis due to herpes zoster?
questions.explanation: Anterior uveitis due to herpes zoster causes intense inflammation that produces atrophy of the iris (atrophic iris) and posterior synechiae, complications characteristic of this viral etiology.

questions.question: What laboratory test confirms the viral etiology in herpes simplex anterior uveitis?
options: Aqueous humor PCR for viral DNA
IgM and IgG serology in peripheral blood
questions.explanation: Aqueous humor polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is the gold standard for confirming the presence of herpes simplex virus DNA in anterior uveitis, with high sensitivity and specificity.

questions.question: What is the key pathophysiological difference between herpes simplex and herpes zoster anterior uveitis?
options: Simplex is primary infection and zoster is reactivation.
Simplex causes keratitis and zoster only uveitis.
Zoster produces retinal necrosis and simplex does not.
Zoster affects the geniculate ganglion and simplex affects the trigeminal ganglion.
questions.explanation: Herpes simplex remains latent in the trigeminal ganglion, while herpes zoster (varicella zoster) does so in the geniculate ganglion; reactivation of each produces anterior uveitis with differences in neural distribution.

questions.question: What is the typical finding in anterior uveitis due to sarcoidosis?
questions.explanation: Granulomatous anterior uveitis due to sarcoidosis is characterized by large, fatty keratic precipitates (in mutton fat), unlike non-granulomatous uveitis.

questions.question: What type of inflammatory reaction predominates in sarcoid uveitis?
questions.explanation: Sarcoidosis produces a non-caseating granulomatous inflammation, which in the eye manifests as granulomatous anterior uveitis with granulomas in the iris and ciliary body.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for active sarcoid anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: The initial management of granulomatous anterior uveitis due to sarcoidosis is topical corticosteroids (prednisolone) together with cycloplegics to break synechiae and relieve pain.

questions.question: What complementary test is key to confirm sarcoidosis in the face of granulomatous uveitis?
questions.explanation: Chest x-ray can show bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, a classic finding of sarcoidosis, and is the initial study of choice when ocular sarcoidosis is suspected.

questions.question: What common complication is associated with chronic granulomatous anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Chronic inflammation and prolonged use of topical corticosteroids in granulomatous anterior uveitis can raise intraocular pressure, causing secondary glaucoma.

questions.question: Which feature of the iris suggests granulomatous uveitis due to sarcoidosis?
questions.explanation: Busacca nodules are granulomatous masses in the stroma of the iris, typical of granulomatous uveitis such as sarcoidosis, unlike Koeppe nodules in the pupillary edge.

questions.question: What anatomical structure adheres to the posterior synechiae of anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Posterior synechiae are adhesions between the posterior surface of the iris and the anterior capsule of the lens, secondary to inflammation in anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of untreated posterior synechiae?
questions.explanation: Extensive posterior synechiae can block the flow of aqueous humor, raising intraocular pressure and triggering secondary angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.question: What drug is used to rupture recent posterior synechiae?
questions.explanation: Mydriatic cycloplegics (such as atropine or cyclopentolate) dilate the pupil and separate the iris from the lens, breaking incipient posterior synechiae and preventing their formation.

questions.question: What clinical sign indicates posterior synechiae on slit lamp examination?
questions.explanation: Posterior synechiae produce an irregular pupil, often apple-seed shaped, due to focal adhesions between the iris and lens.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism favors the formation of posterior synechiae?
questions.explanation: The protein-rich fibrinous exudate, characteristic of inflammation in anterior uveitis, is deposited between the iris and the lens, organizing into adhesions that form the posterior synechiae.

questions.question: What immediate functional consequence do complete posterior synechiae have?
questions.explanation: The posterior synechiae that surround the entire pupil (annular synechiae) prevent pupillary dilation, generating an irreversible paralytic miosis that does not respond to mydriatics.

questions.question: What does Tyndall's sign in the anterior chamber indicate?
questions.explanation: The positive Tyndall sign in the anterior chamber reflects the increase in proteins due to breakdown of the blood-aqueous barrier, typical in anterior uveitis.

questions.question: What cellular finding is observed in the anterior chamber during active uveitis?
questions.explanation: The presence of inflammatory cells (leukocytes) in the anterior chamber is a key sign of activity in anterior uveitis, quantified on slit lamp examination.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of non-infectious anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis is the most common cause of non-infectious anterior uveitis, associated with HLA-B27, frequently presenting as recurrent unilateral disease.

questions.question: What topical treatment is first-line in acute anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate) are the mainstay of treatment in acute anterior uveitis to control inflammation, along with cycloplegics to prevent synechiae.

questions.question: What complication can arise from untreated posterior synechiae in anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Posterior synechiae can block the flow of aqueous humor, raising intraocular pressure and triggering acute glaucoma due to secondary angle closure.

questions.question: What cycloplegic drug is used to rupture synechiae in anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Topical atropine is a long-acting cycloplegic that dilates the pupil and helps rupture posterior synechiae, as well as relieving ciliary muscle spasm.

questions.question: What is the first-line topical corticosteroid in anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Prednisolone acetate 1% is the topical corticosteroid of choice in anterior uveitis due to its high intraocular penetration and anti-inflammatory potency.

questions.question: Which cycloplegic is most frequently used in anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Atropine 1% is the most commonly used cycloplegic in anterior uveitis because it provides prolonged cycloplegia, relieves ciliary muscle spasm, and prevents synechiae.

questions.question: Which of the following is an adverse effect of prolonged use of topical corticosteroids in anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: Ocular hypertension is a known adverse effect of prolonged use of topical corticosteroids, which can induce secondary glaucoma.

questions.question: Why are cycloplegics indicated in the treatment of anterior uveitis?
options: Increase the production of aqueous humor
They dilate the pupil and relieve pain
questions.explanation: Cycloplegics dilate the pupil, paralyze the ciliary muscle and relieve pain caused by ciliary spasm, in addition to preventing the formation of posterior synechiae.

questions.question: Which topical corticosteroid has the lowest risk of raising intraocular pressure?
questions.explanation: Fluorometholone 0.1% is a low-potency topical corticosteroid that produces less elevation of intraocular pressure, useful in patients with a known hypertensive response.

questions.question: What is the typical initial regimen of topical corticosteroids in acute anterior uveitis?
questions.explanation: The frequent initial regimen is every hour while the patient is awake, to achieve a high anti-inflammatory concentration in the anterior chamber, with gradual reduction according to response.
